coredata.dc:title: Enhancement of mass transfer by ultrasound: Application to adsorbent regeneration and food drying/dehydration
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yao, Ye
coredata.dc:description: The physical mechanisms of heat and mass transfer enhancement by ultrasound have been identified by people. Basically, the effect of ‘cavitation’ induced by ultrasound is the main reason for the enhancement of heat and mass transfer in a liquid environment, and the acoustic streaming and vibration are the main reasons for that in a gaseous environment. The adsorbent regeneration and food drying/dehydration are typical heat and mass transfer process, and the intensification of the two processes by ultrasound is of complete feasibility. This paper makes an overview on recent studies regarding applications of power ultrasound to adsorbent regeneration and food drying/dehydration. The concerned adsorbents include desiccant materials (typically like silica gel) for air dehumidification and other ones (typically active carbon and polymeric resin) for water treatment. The applications of ultrasound in the regeneration of these adsorbents have been proved to be energy saving. The concerned foods are mostly fruits and vegetables. Although the ultrasonic treatment may cause food degradation or nutrient loss, it can greatly reduce the food processing time and decrease drying temperature. From the literature, it can be seen that the ultrasonic conditions (i.e., acoustic frequency and power levels) are always focused on during the study of ultrasonic applications. The increasing number of relevant studies argues that ultrasound is a very promising technology applied to the adsorbent regeneration and food drying/dehydration.

row.entry: Study on the use of ultrasound for desorption of phenol from activated carbon and polymeric resin. The lower frequency (40kHz) of ultrasound brings about a better enhancement of desorption rate than the higher frequency (1.44MHz). Acoustic cavitation plays an important role in the desorption process

Ultrasonic frequency is a key parameter for enhancing the regeneration of adsorbent resins. One must not exceed a threshold of acoustic intensity for damaging the adsorbents, and this threshold depends on ultrasound frequency and reactor geometry
Ultrasound can decrease activation energy of surface diffusion due to the intense localized heating and high-pressure shock waves caused by cavitation

The temperature rise due to ultrasonication is an important factor of ultrasound enhanced desorption. Regeneration is more effective at the higher frequency. This could be a thermodynamic or kinetic effect due to cavitation or ultrasonic energy dissipation
The adsorption capacity of phenol on the NKA II resin decreases with the increase of ultrasound intensity
Particles absorb ultrasonic energy effectively if they are in resonance with the ultrasound. Thermal effects are responsible for ultrasonically enhanced desorption with the applied adsorption system
The adsorption capacity of p-chlorophenol on the activated carbon decreases with the increase of ultrasound intensity
Investigate desorption of trichloroethylene (TCE) from granular activated carbon (GAC) by ultrasound. Desorption of TCE by 20kHz ultrasound occurred mainly at a nearby surface of GAC

The amount of p-chlorophenol desorbed from the GAC is higher under the stronger ultrasonic intensity with the exception of that obtained at 1660kHz. A synergetic enhancement of the desorption rate can be obtained when ultrasonic irradiation is coupled with chemical regeneration

Ultrasound not only changes the phase equilibrium state of the NKA-II resin/phenol/water system, but also enhances the mass transfer between phases. The diffusion coefficient of phenol in the resin increases with the ultrasonic intensity increasing

Ultrasound might have an effect on the microporosity of the carbons. The adsorption isotherms are well fitted by the Langmuir equation. Sonication enhanced the initial rate and final amount of desorption compared to the absence of ultrasound

The sono-sorption process is an effective method for the removal of cadmium ion from solution. At a higher acoustic intensity, the lower ratio is more effective for longer time, but at lower intensity, the higher ratio is more effective for shorter time
Ultrasound power has positive effects on Cu(II) desorption and negative effects on Pb(II) desorption

The desorption of 4-CP increases with increasing GAC loading, acoustic power and temperature. The pore diffusion model is suitable for initial time desorption, and the second order surface kinetics model for long time desorption

Ultrasound increases the adsorption rate and decreases the equilibrium time obviously for Cr(VI)/resin system, while for activated carbon, ultrasound only increases the adsorption rate slightly at the initial stage of adsorption

Ultrasound leads to significant enhancement in extent of adsorption and removal percentage of SY and erythrosine from aqueous solutions. Combination of ultrasound with ZnS:Ni-NP-AC increased the dye removal percentage (98%) by using small amount of adsorbent (0.04g) in short time (3.8min)
Ultrasound enhances the removal capacities of Oxytetracycline for all tested carbon nanotubes, but it is not the same case for the removal capacities of Ciprofloxacin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Studies on food dehydration/drying assisted by ultrasound in recent ten years.

row.entry: Develop a prototype ultrasonic dehydration system with vegetable samples for the application of the process in a pre-industrial stage
Study effect of air flow rate, ultrasonic power and mass loading on ultrasound-assisted drying system
Investigate effect of ultrasonic pretreatment on dehydration of bananas. Results showed that that the overall drying time could be reduced by 11%
Air velocity and raw material characteristics play a role in convective drying kinetics assisted by power ultrasound. Power ultrasound increased effective moisture diffusivity at low air velocities

Use ultrasound as pretreatment for drying of mushrooms, Brussels sprouts and cauliflower to reduce drying time. The rehydration properties for ultrasound treated samples were higher than those for untreated samples
High frequency ultrasound had a negative influence on anthocyanins and phenolics
An intensity threshold of ultrasound (about 10W/cm2) was required for the apparent effect of ultrasound on the mass transfer in apple immersed in a sucrose solution
Investigate effect of ultrasound pretreatment on melon tissue structure. Ultrasound treatment increases water diffusivity due to the formation of microscopic channels
Investigate effect of ultrasonic pretreatment on dehydration of pineapple. Results showed that the ultrasound could reduce the overall drying time by 8% (over 1h of air-drying time)
Study drying kinetics of fresh and ultrasonic pretreated banana cv Pacovan using the Fick’s diffusional model and the Page empirical model
Drying kinetics of orange peel was significantly improved by ultrasound. The ultrasonic effects on mass transfer properties were confirmed through microstructural observations
Investigate effect of ultrasound and blanching pretreatments on polyacetylene and carotenoid compounds of hot air and freeze dried carrot discs

Assess moisture loss kinetics and structural changes of eggplant tissue by ultrasonically assisted convective drying. The microstructure of eggplant endocarp was found to be better preserved after the application of a moderate ultrasonic power

Develop a contact ultrasound system for the integration into freeze-drying processes for vegetables. The ultrasound treatment could reduce the drying time, and the ultrasound treatment did not affect product quality. In terms of bulk density, color, ascorbic acid content, and rehydration characteristics

UF: 20kHz
Excitation amplitude by ultrasound: 4.9, 6.0 and 6.7μm

The ultrasound-assisted convective drying was tested by using a screen as product support and sound transmitting surface. Significant improvements of drying characteristics were observed for continuous sonication and intermittent ultrasound at 50% net sonication time
The ultrasound treated apples exhibited between 9% and 11% higher shrinkage, 6–20% lower density, and porosity of 9–14% higher than untreated samples. Meanwhile, it would enhance structural damages
Ultrasound treated for 10min at 60°C provided a higher mechanical strength (P <0.05) to the cell wall structure than blanching for the same time period
Study an ultrasound-assisted sun drying system using a flat bed as product support and two extensional piezoelectric Bolt-clamped 20kHz transducer elements

Study application of ultrasound to osmotic dehydration of guava slices via indirect sonication using an ultrasonic bath system and direct sonication using an ultrasonic probe system. Indirect sonication in osmotic solutions contributed to high water loss and solid gain with acceptable total color change than direct sonication

For indirect sonication: UF: 25kHz
UP: 1.0, 1.75, and 2.5kW;
For direct sonication: UF: 20kHz
UP: 60, 100, and 140W
Assess effect of previous ultrasound and conventional blanching treatments on drying and quality parameters of convective dehydrated carrots

The application of power ultrasound sped up the drying kinetics of apple, reducing the drying time by 77%. However, it results in a greater degradation of polyphenol and flavonoid contents and a reduction of the antioxidant capacity
Explore air-borne ultrasound for drying of strawberry. A diffusion model considering both convective transport and shrinkage was used to describe the drying kinetics
Investigates effect of ultrasound pretreatment on the water state and microstructure of kiwifruit during osmotic dehydration

UF: 35kHz
UI: 84, 97 and 102W/kg for diverse time

Study the convective-intermittent drying of cherries preceded by ultrasonic assisted osmotic dehydration. It was proved that intermittent drying of cherries preceded by ultrasonic-assisted osmotic dehydration contributes to shorter drying time, better color preservation and smaller water activity
Investigate influence of airborne ultrasound conditions on the drying behavior of the model food with a Response Surface Method

The effective diffusivity increased by 1.3–1.8times at 18.5kW/m3 in UI and 1.4–2.2times at 30.8kW/m3 in UI for temperatures between 30 and 70°C. The external mass transfer coefficient increases by 1.1–1.6times at 18.5kW/m3 in UI and 1.2–1.9times at 30.8kW/m3 in UI for temperatures between 30 and 70°C. Meanwhile, ultrasound changed nutrients in the microstructure of apple samples compared to the samples dried without ultrasound
Investigate effect of ultrasound pre-treatment on the drying kinetics of brown seaweed. Drying kinetics has been modeled by using different models
Evaluated the use of ultrasound as a pretreatment for convective drying of Andean blackberry. The drying rate increased by almost five times when samples were treated with ultrasound for 20min
Analyze impact of airborne ultrasound on the drying efficiency of biological materials (Lobo apples)
Investigate influence of ultrasonic power levels applied on freeze drying kinetics

Explore dehydration kinetics of salmon and trout fillets using ultrasonic vacuum drying (USV) method. Under the study conditions, the USV technique could reduce the drying time by 7.4–27.4% compared with vacuum-drying
Analyze the distribution of ultrasonic waves in porous wet materials and to discover the mechanism of ultrasonic interaction with both the solid skeleton and moisture in pores

Investigate effects of high-intensity ultrasound on drying kinetics and antioxidant properties of passion fruit peel. Two diffusion models based on Fick’s second law were used to mathematically describe the drying kinetics

Address air-borne application of power ultrasound in the convective drying of grape skin, a by-product of winemaking. The kinetic intensification was evaluated by modeling the drying kinetics using empirical and diffusion models.
Investigate impact of applied ultrasonic power on the low temperature drying of apple. The maximum shortening of the drying time achieved in this study was 80.3%
Demonstrate mechanisms for improving mass transfer in food with ultrasound technology with two model cases: i.e., the hydration of sorghum grain and the influx of a pigment into melon cylinders

head.ce:title: Enhancement of mass transfer by ultrasound: Application to adsorbent regeneration and food drying/dehydration

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Refrigeration and Cryogenics, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200240, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Address: 800 Dong Chuan Rd., Shanghai 200240, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The physical mechanisms of heat and mass transfer enhancement by ultrasound have been identified by people. Basically, the effect of ‘cavitation’ induced by ultrasound is the main reason for the enhancement of heat and mass transfer in a liquid environment, and the acoustic streaming and vibration are the main reasons for that in a gaseous environment. The adsorbent regeneration and food drying/dehydration are typical heat and mass transfer process, and the intensification of the two processes by ultrasound is of complete feasibility. This paper makes an overview on recent studies regarding applications of power ultrasound to adsorbent regeneration and food drying/dehydration. The concerned adsorbents include desiccant materials (typically like silica gel) for air dehumidification and other ones (typically active carbon and polymeric resin) for water treatment. The applications of ultrasound in the regeneration of these adsorbents have been proved to be energy saving. The concerned foods are mostly fruits and vegetables. Although the ultrasonic treatment may cause food degradation or nutrient loss, it can greatly reduce the food processing time and decrease drying temperature. From the literature, it can be seen that the ultrasonic conditions (i.e., acoustic frequency and power levels) are always focused on during the study of ultrasonic applications. The increasing number of relevant studies argues that ultrasound is a very promising technology applied to the adsorbent regeneration and food drying/dehydration.

ce:section.ce:para: The power ultrasound has been found to be helpful to enhance heat and mass transfer process, and hence, improve systems efficiency. These owe to some special effects induced by power ultrasonic waves when traveling across a medium. Major effects of ultrasound for heat and mass transfer enhancement are acoustic ‘cavitation’ and acoustic streaming [1,2]. There exist other subsequent effects such as ‘heating effect’ due to dissipation of the mechanical energy and ‘sponge effect’ due to alternative compressions and expansions generated by the ultrasonic waves. The heating effect can increase mass diffusivity in a solid medium and promote mass transfer process. The sponge effect produces the release of liquid from the inner part of the particle to the solid surface, and the forces involved in this mechanism can be higher than the surface tension which maintains the water molecules inside the capillaries of the material, creating microscopic channels and making the interchanges of matter easier [3]. From a general viewpoint, all these effects produced by ultrasound could be interesting in applications involving heat or mass transport, decreasing both the external and internal resistance to transport.

As the typical application of power ultrasound in the heat and mass transfer enhancement, the ultrasound-assisted regeneration of adsorbents and food processing have been intensively studied in recent years. The objectives of this paper are to review and discuss recent studies on these topics.

Regeneration of desiccant is a key process in an air dehumidification cycle with the adsorption/absorption method. The air-dehumidification system repeats the adsorption–regeneration–cooling cycle, in which the regeneration conditions will produce great influence on the performance of water vapor adsorption on desiccant [4]. The conventional regeneration method by heating is admitted to be of low energy efficiency due to the relatively higher regeneration temperature of some traditional desiccant materials like silica gel, molecular sieve and so on. The higher regeneration temperature, on one hand, results in more energy dissipation during the cooling process, and on the other hand, it is disadvantageous for the utilization of low-temperature energy sources (such as solar energy and waste heat). To reduce the regeneration temperature of desiccant, some non-heating methods have been put forward for the desiccant regeneration in recently years including ultrasound-assisted method [5–18], electro-osmotic method [19–22] and plasma method [23,24]. This section only presents the recent studies performed in our laboratory on the ultrasound-assisted regeneration of desiccant.

An experimental set-up (as shown in Fig. 1
[6,7]) has been built to investigate the effect of ultrasound on the regeneration of silica gel (an often-used desiccant). The silica gel packed bed is a concentrically cylindrical container. The regeneration air first enters into the inner cylindrical shell, then passes through the silica gel in the bed and finally exhausts outside from the orifices of the outer cylindrical shell.

To evaluate the benefits brought by the power ultrasound, several indicators are suggested for the study. These are the moisture ratio in the silica gel (qs ), the regeneration degree (RD), the enhanced ratio of regeneration (ER) and the energy-saving rate (ESR), which are defined, respectively, as below [6]:

Eq. (6) was derived from the solution of Fick’s second law, in spherical geometry, non-steady state, and with several assumptions including constant surface moisture concentration, uniform initial moisture concentration, radial moisture diffusion and no shrinkage.

In addition, the conditioned regeneration time (ab. CRT), which refers to the time required for obtaining a certain degree of regeneration (RD), is suggested to investigate the reduction of regeneration time due to the presence of ultrasonic radiation. As indicated from Fig. 3
[8], the regeneration time can be significantly reduced as the ultrasound is applied, especially for the lower regeneration temperature.

The energy consumptions are compared between the ‘NU’ (No ultrasound) case and the ‘U’ (ultrasound existing) case under different conditions. The energy consumptions required for different values of RD (RD =0.3, RD =0.4 and RD =0.5) are calculated based on the CRT and total electric power consumption of the heater and the ultrasonic generator. The results of ESR brought by the ultrasound for these cases are presented in Fig. 4
[8], and this manifests that the energy consumption for regeneration can be significantly reduced after the ultrasound is applied to the regeneration. Meanwhile, it can be inferred as well from Fig. 4 that the performance of energy saving brought about by the ultrasound-assisted regeneration is better under the lower regeneration temperatures.

To have a better applications for the convenience of process control, several drying models including the Page (Eq. (7)), the Lewis (Eq. (8)), the Henderson and Pabis (Eq. (9)), the Logarithmic (Eq. (10)) and the Weibull (Eq. (11)) have been examined for describing the regeneration process assisted by ultrasound [9].

The study results show that the Gaussian and the Weibull model perform better than the other models in predicting the dehydrating kinetics of silica gel regeneration assisted by ultrasound. Furthermore, a mathematical model has been developed to illustrate the ultrasound-assisted regeneration process of silica gel packed bed whose physical model is presented in Fig. 5
[10] where Ta, w and u stand for temperature, humidity and flow velocity of air, respectively. The air passes through the bed in horizontal direction (X), and the ultrasound waves propagate in vertical direction (Y). Some assumptions have been made for the mathematical model development mainly including: (1) the silica gel bed is considered as a porous-media one that is packed with numerous rigid solid particles; (2) the acoustic propagation is an adiabatic one-dimensional process; (3) the influence of air flow and temperature gradient on the acoustic standing waves in the bed is negligible; (4) the heat and mass transfer process between the air and the silica gel in the bed is treated as one-dimensional one, and mainly controlled by convectional way; and (5) the states of air in a microelement is assumed to be steady in a small period of time during the regeneration. The mathematical model consists of two parts: one is about the fundamental heat and mass transfer process in the silica gel packed bed, and the other is about the sound propagation in porous media. The heat and mass transfer model is established based on the first law of energy and mass conservation, and the sound propagation model in porous media is developed by using linearized-acoustic continuity, state and momentum equations. The detailed information about the modeling equations can be found in Ref. [10]. The theoretical model allows us to have a comprehensive analysis on the ultrasound-assisted regeneration under any conditions [14].

The mechanism of the regeneration enhancement brought by high-intensity ultrasound is illustrated in Fig. 6
[16]. The power ultrasound can cause high frequency vibration of the air near the silica gel (micro-‘vibration effect’), which leads to turbulence near the surface of silica gel and promotes heat and mass transfer process. Meanwhile, part of the ultrasonic energy will be directly absorbed and bring about a temperature rise (‘heating effect’) in the silica gel, which will increase the water vapor pressure in equilibrium with the silica gel. The respective contribution ratio of the two effects to the regeneration enhancement has been discussed in the Ref. [16]. It was found that the regeneration enhancement brought by ultrasound was not a simple sum of the contributions of ‘vibration effect’ and ‘heating effect’. The synergy of the two effects could produce extra regeneration enhancement. The contribution ratio of the ‘synergistic effect’ increased as the regeneration air humidity and the ultrasonic power increased and the air temperature and velocity decreased. In addition, the contribution ratios of the two effects would be affected by regeneration conditions, e.g., the contribution ratio of the ‘micro-vibration effect’ might dominate under the higher regeneration temperature or under the lower ultrasonic power level.

In a recent study [17], the mechanism of ultrasound-enhanced regeneration has been further illustrated by field synergy principle and mass diffusion theory. The average synergy degree (
κ
¯
, which only considers intersection angle between the velocity and the temperature gradient) and the average overall synergy degree (
κ
¯
overall
, which considers local values of velocity and temperature gradient based on
κ
¯
) in terms of the near wall region were suggested for the analysis, and they were obtained based on the k-omega model which is suitable for the near wall free-shear flow velocity predicting. The local synergy degrees between the temperature and the velocity field have been obtained as shown in Fig. 7 . As seen from Fig. 7, the ultrasonic mechanical effect can significantly enlarge the synergy degrees between the temperature and the velocity field around the particle, and this can be used to explain the enhancement of convective heat and mass transfer on the gas side due to the mechanical effect of ultrasound.

A moisture diffusion model was developed as well to investigate moisture diffusivity in a silica gel particle during the regeneration with and without ultrasound. The variations of moisture diffusivity against the regeneration time are presented in Fig. 8
[17]. The results manifest that the moisture diffusivity in the silica gel particle under the ultrasonic irradiation is always higher than that without ultrasonic irradiation. The increased moisture diffusivity by ultrasound should owe to the ultrasonic heating effect that brings about temperature increase in medium.

For liquid desiccants, the cavitation effect of power ultrasound spays the solution into numerous tiny droplets with the size of 40–80μm (known as ‘ultrasonic atomization’), and this can improve the regeneration rate of liquid desiccants through enlarging the contact area between the air and the desiccant solute instead of increasing the solution temperature.

Fig. 9 gives the effect of droplet size on the mass transfer coefficient of water droplet exposed to an airstream at 40°C. It indicates that the mass transfer coefficient of droplet increases greatly with the decrease of the droplet size, especially within the range of 0–50μm; and the influence of droplet size on the mass transfer coefficient tends to be less significant as the size of droplet increases. By using the ultrasonic atomization, the droplet size can be easily controlled within tens of micrometers, and this will enhance greatly the moisture transfer and increase the regeneration rate of liquid desiccant.

An experimental system has been built to investigate the liquid desiccant regeneration with the ultrasonic atomizing [13]. As shown in Fig. 10 , the experimental system mainly contains an atomizing chamber, an ultrasonic atomizer, a thermostatic solution tank, a fan and wire mesh demisters. The hot-and-weak desiccant solution is atomized by an ultrasonic atomizer with the frequency of 30kHz, and sprayed into the atomizing chamber in which the regeneration air passes through the atomizing chamber from top to bottom. Most of these droplets fall into the strong solution tank below the atomizing chamber. The droplets entrained by the air flow will be intercepted by wire mesh demisters at the outlet of the chamber. Based on this experimental setup, a theoretical model has been developed [13,18].

To evaluate the performance of the regenerator, the three parameters, i.e., regeneration rate (RR), regeneration effectiveness (RE) and specific energy consumption (SEC: J/(kgwater)), have been recommended, which are expressed as below [13,18]:

are air humidity (in kg/(kgdryair)) in equilibrium with the desiccant solution entering and leaving the regenerator, respectively; E reg is energy consumption (in J) for a specific time period of regeneration, which includes the energy consumption by the solution heater, the ultrasonic atomizer, the fan and the solution pump; m reg is mass of moisture desorption during the regeneration in kg.

The RR reflects directly the dehydration rate of desiccant solution in a regeneration process. The higher RR means the larger capacity of dehumidification in a specific time. In a real desiccant system, the minimum RR must be required for actual operation. Normally, the RR can be improved through altering the regeneration conditions (e.g., increase the temperature of the solution or the regeneration air) and the physical structure of regenerator (e.g., increase the contact area or contact time between the solution and regeneration air). The regeneration effectiveness (RE) is closely related to the structure of the regenerator and the method of regeneration. The RE reflects the regeneration degree of the liquid desiccant by the regenerator. The higher RE means that the heat and mass transfer between the solution and the regeneration air in the regenerator is proceeded more fully.

By using the proposed model, influences of inlet solution temperature and concentration on the performances of the ultrasound-atomizing regenerator have been discussed in Ref. [18], as shown in Figs. 11 and 12
. It can be seen from Fig. 11 that the regeneration rate (RR), the regeneration effectiveness (RE) and the variations of the solution mass concentration all increase linearly with the inlet solution temperature increasing. The SEC has a significant increase when the inlet solution temperature rises from 50 to 65 °C. Afterwards, the increase rate of the SEC against the inlet solution temperature becomes increasingly smaller. Fig. 12 shows that the regeneration rate (RR), the regeneration effectiveness (RE) and the variations of the solution mass concentration all decrease with the increase of the inlet solution concentration, and it is opposite for the specific energy consumption (SEC). This indicates that the lower inlet solution concentration will be conductive to improving the energy efficiency of the ultrasound-atomizing regenerator.

Comparisons of regeneration rate of LiCl solution between the ultrasound-atomizing regenerator and the conventional packed one under different ambient air temperatures have been made, as shown in Fig. 13
[18]. The two regenerators share the same dimension of space for the regeneration (0.15m in the height, and 0.2m in the cross sectional diameter), and the inlet solution temperature and mass concentration is identically taken as 60°C and 0.30, respectively. The results manifest that the ultrasound-atomizing regenerator will have an obviously higher regeneration rate than the conventional packed one under different ambient air conditions.

Although the ultrasound-atomizing regenerator has a better performance than the conventional packed one, it is still faced with some challenges such as carryover of solution droplets, low utilization efficiency of reaction chamber space and so on. The future work will be focused on the optimization of the ultrasound-atomizing regeneration including the operation conditions and the structure of regenerator.

The other commonly used adsorbents are activated carbon, polymeric resin, and etc. They are mostly used for extraction of many organic toxic and colored pollutants in industrial waste water treatment [28]. For the sake of cost saving and environmental protection, the absorbents are normally regenerated for reuse [29]. Usually, the thermal method is adopted for the regeneration of adsorbent [30], but it is limited by its high costs that arise from high temperature supplies needed. In recent two decades, there have been many efforts to come up with the new method of adsorbent regeneration with ultrasound that is potentially superior to the thermal method. Table 1 summarizes the studies on regeneration of adsorbents by ultrasound.

Summarized from the literature in Table 1, the acoustic frequency and intensity are the two key parameters for the ultrasound applications in the regeneration of adsorbents. In the study on the use of ultrasound for desorption of phenol from activated carbon and polymeric resin [31], the lower frequency (40kHz) of ultrasound was shown to have a better enhancement of desorption rate than the higher frequency (1.44MHz). Hamdaoui et al. [39] performed an experimental study on the desorption of p-chlorophenol from exhausted activated carbon under different acoustic frequencies (i.e., 21, 800 and 1660kHz) and in silent conditions with stirring. Fig. 14 presents some key data on the amount of p-chlorophenol desorbed from activated carbon (GAC) against time under different conditions. The results in Fig. 14 manifest that the amount of p-chlorophenol (p-CP) desorbed from the GAC is higher under the stronger ultrasonic intensity with the exception of that obtained at 1660kHz. The comparison of desorption experiments at high frequencies shows that the rates of desorption at 800kHz are much higher than corresponding rates observed at 1660kHz. When the ultrasonic frequency attains within the megahertz region, the production of cavitation by ultrasound in liquids decreases, and this may be the reason for the decrease of desorption amount of p-CP. In the study by Breitbach et al. [32], the effects of ultrasound on the desorption process of a micro-porous polymeric resin Amberlite CR 1320 Ca were investigated. The experimental results indicated that ultrasound could promote desorption of adsorbed molecules and enhance pore diffusion in sorption process, and the both effects increased with the increase of acoustic frequency and intensity.

Different researchers have different opinions on the mechanism of ultrasonic desorption of adsorbents. Rege et al. [31] and Schueller et al. [33] held that the enhancement of desorption rate by ultrasound owed to the acceleration of mass transport by cavitation and acoustic vortex microstreaming. The generation of “hot spots” due to cavitation and acoustic microstreaming in the pores seemed to enable the rupture of the strong sorbate–surface bonds and improve desorption rate when an ultrasonic field was applied. Breitbach et al. [34,36] thought that the most important factor of ultrasound enhanced desorption might be the temperature rise due to ultrasonic energy dissipation. Hamdaoui et al. [37] indicated that the improvement of the desorption rate was attributed to high-speed microjets (microstreaming) and high-pressure shock waves produced by acoustic cavitation. According to these opinions, the mechanism of ultrasonic desorption of adsorbents may be illustrated as below: when ultrasonic wave contacts surface of adsorbent in different phases or forms, ultrasonic crest with a given angle, forces of compressions will be generated on the surface or in the area of wave collision; during the reflection of waves there is usually the formation of small “cavitation bubbles” that are of several tens of nanometer in size and exist for a short time (i.e., up to hundredths of a second), and the bursting of the “cavitation bubbles” causes a rise in local temperature up to thousands degrees while the local pressure reaches up to hundreds of bars [48], which causes acceleration of thermal motion of adsorbates and helps to separate the adsorbates from the surface of adsorbent.

Ultrasonic cavitation may produce a destructive effect on friable solids, and hence, the maximum intensity at the optimum frequency should be found so as not to destroy the adsorbents when treated with ultrasound. This problem has been paid particular attention to by some researchers. In Rege et al.’s study [31], the experiment at 40kHz revealed that considerable pulverization of the activated carbon occurred. The attrition of the activated carbon could be prevented by operating at a higher frequency (e.g., 1.44MHz) and with intensity below the threshold of the pulverization of carbon. By applying a higher frequency, however, much lower power can be provided by ultrasonic transducer compared with the lower frequency. Rege et al. [31] also investigated the resin stability in the presence of ultrasound. According to the structural stability study of Amberlite XAD-4 and Dowex Optipore L-493 resins to withstand the abrasive cavitational effects of ultrasound at 40kHz, the Dowex Optipore resin was stable under the Irradiation with ultrasound at 40kHz (120W) for 2h. Breitbach et al. [32] tested the stability of the microporous polymeric resin Amberlite CR 1320 Ca under ultrasonic irradiation with different frequencies (40.6 and 350.5kHz) and intensities for one hour. As shown in Fig. 15 , ultrasound with a frequency of 40.6kHz destroys the particles a lot at very low power (36W), and that with a frequency of 350.5kHz erodes the resin very little even the ultrasonic power attains 109W.

Dehydration is a very important process in food industry that is used for food preservation. It is the process of removing moisture content from foods. Dehydration can be taken to mean the same as drying but in some cases the two may differ depending on the level of water content [49]. Food with a mass fraction of water less than 2.5% is said to be dehydrated while food with mass fraction of water greater than 2.5% is said to be dried [50]. The drying of food is carried out at mild temperatures (i.e., from 40 to 70°C) to minimize food degradation [51]. The drying period depends on the type of food, the desired final moisture content, initial moisture content, and the temperature used for drying. Dehydration methods can be thermal and mechanical. In mechanical dehydration the material is pressed or centrifuged [52]. In thermal dehydration, the liquid in food is evaporated due to the heat energy. The strength of liquid attachment on the material determines the dehydration method to be applied. The mechanical dehydration is suitable where moisture is weakly attached to the material, while thermal drying can be applied where the liquid is strongly or weakly attached to the material [3]. The most commonly used method is hot air drying but it has one disadvantage of producing changes which can lead to degradation of food quality.

Recently, a large number of researchers have been involved in the application of ultrasound to food drying. It has been found that the mass transport kinetics can be increased by using pressure waves such as high-intensity ultrasound [53]. The high-intensity air-bone ultrasound causes variations in pressure at liquid/gas interface thus increasing rate of evaporation of moisture. In addition, the ultrasonic waves can reduce the boundary layer across which diffusion takes place, which results in the reduction of resistance to mass transfer at the interface between dried sample and hot air [54]. A substantial decrease (up to 15%) in the boundary layer thickness has been found when conventional spray drying of blood plasma was complemented by high-intensity sound at f =15kHz and I =155dB [55]. The drying method of hot air combined with acoustic radiation allows the utilization of lower temperatures and may be appropriate for drying heat sensitive materials. The positive results that have so far been obtained continue to validate the use of ultrasonic vibrations in direct contact with food samples.

Fuente-Blanco et al. [56] designed a direct contact ultrasonic dehydration system with a rectangular vibrating plate (as shown in Fig. 16 ), which was looking to approach industrial treatments of different products. The system was composed of ultrasonic power generator, data acquisition unit, treatment chamber, signals and power conditioner and a PC to monitor and control the process parameters. The drying process was performed in the treatment chamber. The ultrasonic transducer was located at the upper part of the system. The vacuum chamber was fixed parallel to the transducer plate. The food sample was placed in the upper porous surface. A pneumatically controlled pressure cylinder was fixed at the bottom of the vacuum chamber, which allowed the application of a constant force at the interface transducer samples. The removal of internal moisture of the food sample was increased by use of a forced-air generator with controllable flow-rate and temperature. The PWM (pulse width modulation) controlled the velocity of air. The components of the ultrasonic power generator driving the ultrasonic transducer included a power amplifier, an impedance matching unit, and a system for controlling resonance frequency.

A contact ultrasound system for the integration into freeze-drying processes for vegetables has been developed by Schössler et al. [57], as shown in Fig. 17 . The ultrasound system consisted of the ultrasound processor with two sonotrodes attached to a stainless steel ring through a titanium thread bolt. A stainless steel screen mesh with an aperture of 500μm was soldered on the ring. The ultrasound processor was driven by an ultrasound generator with adjustable amplitude level. The ultrasound treatment was featured by the excitation amplitude at the contact point of sonotrode and screen frame, which was measured by laser interferometry.

Bantle and Eikevik [58] developed an ultrasound-assisted drying system combined with a heat pump as shown in Fig. 18 . Two condensers were employed to keep the drying air from overheating. The first condenser transferred the excess energy from the compressors to a district heating system, while the second was used to reheat the drying air to the required drying temperature. The drying air from the tunnel was first cooled down in the evaporator, and the water was removed by condensation below a certain temperature. The air was later reheated to its initial temperature in the second condenser, gaining back its own heat. Since water was removed in the evaporator, the relative humidity of the air was lower after passing through the condenser and could be used again at the inlet of the tunnel.

Kouchakzadeh designed a solar flat bed combined with ultrasound transducer for pistachios drying, as shown in Fig. 19
[59]. The bed was equipped with oad sensor (SM601, Sewha, Inc., Korea) and two bolt clamped 20kHz ultrasound transducer. The load sensor was situated at the middle of the plate that contacts the ground surface with the basis, and the ultrasound transducers were placed at opposite the corners. The driving transducer consisted of an extensional piezoelectric Bolt-clamped Langevin type transducer element (SMBLTD50F20 HA) with aluminum body.

Gamboa-Santos et al. [60] designed a prototype of an ultrasonic-assisted convective dryer to dry strawberries, as shown in Fig. 20 . The ultrasonic device included a cylindrical vibrating radiator driven by a piezoelectric transducer (21.8kHz), which generated a high-intensity ultrasonic field in the air medium where the samples were placed. A high power ultrasound generator, an impedance matching unit and a digital power meter regulated and measured the electrical parameters of the acoustic signal (voltage, intensity, phase, frequency and power). Santacatalina et al. [61] also developed a similar ultrasonically assisted drying system to study the ultrasonically enhanced low-temperature drying of apple.

The transducers are the key device for the application of ultrasound, which converts electric energy into mechanical energy in the form of ultrasonic vibrations. The transducers are attached to the vibrating system whose function consists of transmitting the vibration from the transducer to the medium. For the liquid applications [62–67], the most commonly used systems are baths and probe-type systems. In the ultrasonic baths, several transducers are usually attached to the bottom of a metallic tank transmitting the vibration to the contained liquid, and in the probe systems, ultrasound is directly applied by a vibrating ‘horn’. For the solid–gas environment, the main challenge of the application of ultrasound is the transmission of the acoustic wave from the surface of emitter to the samples because the air is a high attenuating medium that absorbs the acoustic energy. Moreover, the high impedance difference between the air and the solid produces reflection of a high proportion of the generated acoustic energy, preventing the acoustic energy from transferring to the solids to be treated. Therefore, it is very important to study the mechanisms of ultrasonic wave transmission in gas media in order to have a better application. It is noted that some recent studies [56,68–71] have been made to alleviate these problems by developing a powerful source of airborne ultrasound (like stepped plate ultrasonic transducers) that can achieve a more efficient transmission of energy to the material and by developing vibration chambers apt for the transmission of air borne ultrasonic energy. Some promising results of this system will be addressed in the following sections.

Drying kinetics was usually modeled by using the well-known diffusion model [72]. Eqs. Eq. (18) has been used to describe the moisture loss kinetics of eggplant cylinders by Puig et al. [74]. In their study, the following hypotheses have been assumed to solve the diffusion equation:
•
The initial moisture content and temperature are uniform inside the sample.
•
The mass transport is addressed as a moving boundary problem since the sample volume changes during drying due to the water loss.
•
The solid symmetry in both radial and axial pathways.
•
A significant external resistance to water transfer due to the low air velocity (e.g., 1m/s).

The above diffusion problem can be numerically solved by the implicit finite difference method [75]. At the same time, the separation of variables method can be used if the following hypotheses are stated [76]: (1) isotropic solid, (2) uniform initial moisture content, (3) solid symmetry, (4) negligible external resistance, and (5) constant sample volume. The theoretical models are somewhat complex and difficult to apply to real situations. Therefore, some empirical models have been developed to describe the ultrasound-assisted drying processes with specific conditions. They are summarized in Table 2 .

The studies on food dehydration/drying assisted by ultrasound in recent ten years are summarized in Table 3 . As indicated from these studies, the ultrasonic frequency employed for food dehydration/drying generally ranges from 20 to 40kHz. In the following section, some important results on the ultrasound-assisted drying/dehydration for food are reviewed.
(1)
Influence of ultrasound on the mass transport
The effects induced by power ultrasound during traveling across a medium are diverse and their relative importance largely depends on the characteristics of the medium. In liquids, the power ultrasound may produce ‘cavitation’ which inevitably intensifies the mass transport. In gas media, the power ultrasound may produce pressure variations or microstirring which can affect the mass transfer through reducing the boundary layer thickness. In solid media, alternative compressions and expansions generated by the ultrasonic waves produce sponge effect that lead to the release of liquid from the inner part of the particle to the solid surface and the entry of fluid from outside. Meanwhile, the thermal effect due to acoustic energy dissipation brings about a temperature rise of media and enhances internal mass transfer in solid. The main applications of ultrasound in food drying or dehydration are linked to the effects it has on mass transfer process which mostly occurs in solid–liquid and solid–gas systems.
Numerous studies have proved that the power ultrasound could significantly intensify both internal and external mass transfer process. Fernandes et al. found that the application of ultrasound increased the effective water diffusivity in banana, melons and pineapples by 14.4% [51], 39.3% [63] and 64.3% [64], respectively, during the air-drying process. They thought ultrasound treatment could increase water diffusivity due to the formation of microscopic channels which reduce the water diffusion resistance and make easier for water to diffuse during air-drying. Jambrak et al. [78] investigated the effect of power ultrasound on the effective diffusion coefficient (De ) of several vegetables (i.e., Brussels sprouts, mushrooms and cauliflower) during the drying/dehydration process. Their results manifested that values of De were almost doubled after ultrasound treatment with 20kHz probe for Brussels sprouts and cauliflower. Ortuño et al. [85] carried out experimental study on the convective drying kinetics of orange peel slabs (thickness 5.95±0.41mm) at 40°C and 1m/s with and without power ultrasound application. Results showed that ultrasonic application affected both internal and external mass transport. Effective moisture diffusivity (which means a faster water movement through the solid under acoustic energy) increased from 4.04×10−9 (without ultrasound) to 6.13×10−9 m2/s (with ultrasound). In the case of mass transfer coefficient, the power ultrasound brought about an improvement from 1.17×10−3 (without ultrasound) to 2.43×10−3 [kgwater/(m2 s)] (with ultrasound). The authors explained that all the effects induced by ultrasound, including pressure variations, oscillating velocities and microstreaming, would reduce the boundary layer thickness and increase the water transfer to air phase, which might be the reason for the mass transfer coefficient improvement by ultrasound. The effects on mass transfer properties were confirmed as well from microstructural observations. In Puig et al.’s study [74], the De value of eggplant for ultrasonically assisted drying at 90W was found almost one order of magnitude higher than the value obtained in conventional drying experiments (AIR), and the external mass transfer coefficient also had an big increase after the ultrasound was applied. The identified value increased from 1.79×10−3 (without ultrasound) to 3.29×10−3 kgw/(m2 s) for ‘AIR+US-45W’ experiments and reached 5.89×10−3 kgw/(m2 s) for ‘AIR+US-90W’ experiments. In authors’ opinion, the improvement of external mass transfer coefficient should owe to the reduction of the boundary layer thickness by a mechanical stirring of the gas medium that ultrasonic waves produce at the interfaces. Kek et al. [89] applied ultrasound pre-osmotic treatment prior to hot-air drying of guava slices. It was found that the ultrasonically pretreatment decreased the drying time by 17–33%, increased the effective diffusivity by 18–35%, and increased the drying rate constants of guava slices by 37–42%. Rodríguez et al. [96] investigated the effects of the power ultrasound on the effective mass diffusion coefficient (De : m2/s) and the external mass transfer coefficient (k: kgwater/(m2 s)) during the drying of apple under different temperatures. Under the ultrasonic power level of 18.5kW/m3, the increase of De was of 1.6, 1.8 and 1.3times, respectively, for the drying air temperatures at 30, 50 and 70°C, and the corresponding increase of k was 1.6, 1.4 and 1.1times. The increase of effective moisture diffusivity (De ) and external mass transfer coefficient (k) due to the use of power ultrasound in the fruits or vegetables were also observed in the other studies like Ref. [61,88,100].
(2)
Parametric study
As illustrated in the previous section, the application of ultrasound during food drying increases the kinetics of dehydration, affecting both the internal and the external resistance. The magnitude of the effect of ultrasound on the food dehydration or drying will be affected by the process variables, such as acoustic power and frequency, air velocity, air temperature, moisture ratio and so on.
It is easy to understand that the higher power input will lead to the better effect of ultrasound on the drying process. Fig. 21 clearly shows a direct increase of the drying effect with the acoustic intensity in the ultrasonically drying of carrot [56]. Rodríguez et al. [96] studied the influence of power ultrasound with two intensities (i.e., 18.5 and 30.8kW/m3) on the drying kinetics of apple. Their experimental results showed that the higher intensity of ultrasound brought about the bigger increase of effective diffusivity and external mass transfer coefficient. Some researchers [68,69,91] found that an ultrasonic intensity threshold was required for obtaining some evidence of the effect of ultrasound on the kinetic parameters of the process. Above the threshold, the relationship between the kinetic parameters and the applied ultrasonic power was linear.
The frequency of the ultrasonic waves determines the rate of cavitation or alternative compressions and expansions, which influences the drying rates of food. A comparison of low frequency 35kHz and high frequency 130kHz in ultrasound-assisted osmotic dehydration of cranberries indicated that the lower ultrasonic frequency was more favorable for the food dehydration, decreasing hardness and color retention [103]. This agrees with what was reported by Garcia-Noguera et al. [104] while comparing the effect of ultrasonic frequency on the ultrasound-assisted osmotic dehydration of strawberries. They found that the rate of drying of strawberries at 25kHz was faster than that at 40kHz. Jambrak et al. [78] held that the ultrasonic frequency used in power ultrasound applications should be usually around 20kHz, because higher frequencies result in an increase of energy absorption by the solution and, as a consequence, the wave does not penetrate as deeply into the solid.
Fig. 22 gives the effect of the air flow rate (0.6–10m/s) on the ultrasonic drying of lemon peel slices [68]. The B parameter on the y axis is inversely linked to drying rate. As shown in Fig. 22, at low air velocities (0.6m/s) power ultrasound increases the drying rate, however, at the higher air velocities (>2m/s) the effect of ultrasound on drying kinetics diminishes and becomes negligible. A similar regular pattern was observed as well by Garcia-Perez et al. [76], e.g., during the carrot and persimmon drying, power ultrasound increased the effective moisture diffusivities only at low air velocities, and the improvement was negligible at high air velocities. This phenomenon may be explained by the following reasons [68,76]: Firstly, the air velocity disturbs the acoustic field reducing the energy present in the medium, i.e., the remaining amount of acoustic energy was not enough to affect the mass transfer process at high air velocities; Secondly, power ultrasound mainly affects external mass transfer resistance in the solid–gas system, but the mass transfer during the drying is mostly controlled by internal resistance.
Rodríguez et al. [96] investigated the relative effect of ultrasound on the external mass transfer under different drying temperatures (i.e., 30, 50 and 70°C). The results are presented in Fig. 23 , which indicate that the magnitude of the effect of ultrasound on the external mass transfer decreases with the drying air temperature increasing. The relative effect of ultrasound depends on the mass transfer resistance affected. If the mass transfer resistance is low, the effect of ultrasound application is also low. It can be inferred from Fig. 23 that the mass transfer resistance is lower under the higher drying air temperature.
The moisture content in the food is another important parameter that produces great influence on the ultrasound-assisted drying process. As seen from Fig. 24
[58], the drying rate with ultrasound is at least two times higher than that without ultrasound during the initial stages of the process in which the moisture content is relatively higher. However, as the process continues, the effect of ultrasound on the drying rate becomes less and less significant.
(3)
Influence of ultrasound on food quality
Different nutrient elements have been selected to evaluate the quality and bioactivity of dried or dehydrated products, including antioxidant, phenolic content, sugar, titratable acidity, anthocyanins, polyacetylenes, carotenoids, vitamin C, flavonoid, anthocyanins and so on. In addition, some physical properties are also concerned by people like shrinkage, density, porosity, mechanical strength, water content and color change.
Carlos et al. [67] investigated antioxidants retention in Andean blackberry after the application of ultrasound as pretreatment of convective drying. They pointed out the application of ultrasound increased the migration of antioxidants compounds to the liquid medium, and this reduced the retention of these components in the fruit, and hence led to the loss of its functional quality. However, the application of ultrasound as pretreatment before convective drying allowed a reduction on processing time and the use of lower drying temperatures, which was an improvement to traditional processes in terms of energy consumption and functional quality of the product. Fig. 25 shows the effect of variables on antioxidant retention obtained by Carlos et al. [67]. It can be seen from Fig. 24 that the effect of time of sonication on the antioxidant retention is more important at higher values of amplitude.
The effect which the application of ultrasound during drying has on the total phenolic content (TPC) and Antioxidant capacity (AC) of extracts has been evaluated by Santacatalina et al. [61] and Cruz et al. [71]. Conclusion has been made that ultrasound would result in the degradation of polyphenols with lower AC. It was observed that TPC would reduce by 18.4–31.0% and AC would reduce by 5.9–9.9% after the ultrasound was applied to the drying of grape skin [71]. The degradation caused by ultrasonic application should not be related to its heating effect since temperature positively affects TPC and AC [95]. Cruz et al. thought Ultrasound application reduced the antioxidant potential probably due to oxidase activation and cell degradation [71]. However, the application of ultrasound increased both the effective diffusivity and the mass transfer coefficient, particularly at the mildest temperatures like 40 and 50°C. At these temperatures, ultrasound application reduced the drying time, and hence, limits the oxidation reactions, helping to maintain the antioxidant capacity [99]. Therefore, the drying conditions should be further optimized when the power ultrasound is introduced to the fruit drying process.
The ultrasonic treatment may be an interesting process to produce low sugar dried fruits. Fernandes et al. [51] found that the amount of sugars lost during the process was 21.3% of the reducing sugars of the banana after 30min in ultrasonic bath. In their another study on the drying of pineapples with ultrasonic pretreatment [64], the amount of sugars lost during the process was 21.7% of the reducing sugars of the fruit after 10min in ultrasonic bath, increasing to 23.2% after 30min in ultrasonic bath.
Ultrasound may change the mechanical properties of dried product. Day et al. [94] found that carrot samples ultrasound treated for 10min at 60°C provided a higher mechanical strength (P <0.05) to the cell wall structure than blanching for the same time period. Fig. 26 presents typical compression curves of carrots (after retorting) that had been pretreated by low temperature low time blanching (LTB) or US with or without Ca. It can be inferred from Fig. 25 that both blanching and ultrasound treatment have the benefit to enhance the mechanical properties of retorted carrots, and the addition of Ca ions provided further synergistic effects [94].
Schössler et al. [57] integrated the power ultrasound into freeze drying processes for vegetables (like bell pepper). No differences were found in bulk density, color, ascorbic acid content or rehydration characteristics of the ultrasound dried samples (bell pepper) in comparison to those freeze-dried without the application of ultrasound although the ultrasound treated samples showed slightly lower water content [57]. However, in the other work by Nowacka et al. [93], considerable differences in the density and porosity of the dried apple with and without ultrasonic application were confirmed by scanning electron microscopy image analysis of the investigated tissue. The ultrasound treated apples exhibited between 9% and 11% higher shrinkage, 6–20% lower density, and porosity of 9–14% higher than untreated samples. It indicates that the influence of ultrasound on the physical properties of the dried product largely depends on the material to be processed.

Recent research literature has been intensively reviewed regarding the applications of ultrasound in adsorbent regeneration and food drying/dehydration which are essentially heat and mass transfer process. In different applications, the leading ultrasonically induced effects are different for illustrating the cause of heat and mass transfer enhancement. For the regeneration of solid desiccant, the effect of thermal heating and mechanical vibration induced by ultrasound are the main mechanism for the enhancement, and for the regeneration of liquid desiccant, the acoustic ‘cavitation’ induced by ultrasound is responsible for the enhancement, while for the regeneration of adsorbents used in water treatment, the main reasons for the enhancement by ultrasound are the acoustic ‘cavitation’ and acoustic streaming. In the food drying/dehydration applications, the direct effects induced by ultrasound are related to the ‘sponge effect’ and inertial flux which can keep micro channels and pores unobstructed, facilitating mass transfer, and the indirect effect is related to micro channel formation due to the acoustic ‘cavitation’. When ultrasound waves travel through the product, the phenomenon of ‘cavitation’ takes place in the water inside or outside the product cells, resulting in cell and tissue disruption and the consequent formation of cavities and micro channels which is believed to be the main effect of the ultrasound technology in enhancing the mass transfer during the food drying or dehydration. Although the application of power ultrasound in food drying can reduce the drying time, it may cause the loss of some nutrient elements of fruits and vegetables such as antioxidant, phenolic content, sugar and so on. Meanwhile, it will produce influence on some physical properties of food like shrinkage, density, porosity, mechanical strength, water content and color change.

The ultrasonic frequency is the key acoustic parameter that greatly affects the role of ultrasound in real applications. For the regeneration of solid desiccant (typically like silica gel), the acoustic frequency employed in these studies ranges from 20 to 35kHz, while for the regeneration of adsorbents used in water treatment (typically like activated carbon and polymeric resin), the studied acoustic frequency has a very wide range (from 40 to 1660kHz), and the effect of ultrasound on the adsorbents becomes increasingly strong as the acoustic frequency increases. The ultrasonic frequency employed in the food drying or dehydration generally ranges from 20 to 40kHz.

Although very promising results have been reported in the literature, most of these works deal with fundamental studies at the laboratory scale involving academic setups and the scaleup of the ultrasonic technology to pilot or industrial scale has not been yet deeply investigated. As an emerging research area, there still exist some technological challenges in real practice. The crucial problems concerned are related to the efficient ultrasonic generator and transducer, and the impedance matching between the ultrasonic transducer and the environmental medium. It is then expected that the combined efforts of acousticians, chemical and mechanical engineers will also help to design a proper ultrasonic transducer particularly for some specific occasions in order to achieve as good as possible effect. For example, the ultrasonic transducers developed for solid desiccant regeneration should be featured by good impedance matching with air, large amplitude of vibration, and high power capacity as well as extensive radiation area. Meanwhile, the acoustic frequency and power of transducer should be cautiously designed according to the acoustic characteristics (including the acoustic impedance, attenuation and absorption) and the working conditions. The other challenge confronted is heat dissipation of the ultrasonic transducers during their running. The temperature of the ultrasonic transducer may be very high if the heat dissipation cannot be rejected in time, and this will greatly reduce the ultrasonic transducer’s working efficiency and result in the breakdown of the equipment. To solve such problem, on one hand, we should develop a cooling system particularly for the high-intensity ultrasonic transducer; and on the other hand, we should develop a high-performance piezoelectric ceramic material which is featured by large piezoelectric constant, low dielectric loss and small temperature coefficient so as to reduce the heat dissipation. Owing to the difficulties to meet such a technological challenge, it is then expected that the combined efforts of acousticians, chemical and mechanical engineers will also contribute to promoting the application of ultrasonic technology in the adsorbent regeneration and food drying/dehydration.
